news.title_en: Newegg Launches North America Exclusive AMD Ryzen™ 7 7700X3D Processor, A Lower-Cost Way Into High-End Gaming Amid Rising Memory Prices
news.title_zh: Newegg Launches North America Exclusive AMD Ryzen™ 7 7700X3D Processor, A Lower-Cost Way Into High-End Gaming Amid Rising Memory Prices
news.summary_en: DIAMOND BAR, Calif., July 16, 2026--Newegg (Newegg Commerce, Inc., NASDAQ: NEGG), a global leader in computer and technology products, today announced the exclusive launch of the AMD Ryzen 7 7700X3D Processor, a new gaming processor available only at Newegg, starting July 16. Newegg will celebrate the launch the next day with AMD Game On, a live event at the Newegg Gamer Zone on July 17.

Newegg will celebrate the launch the next day with AMD Game On, a live event at the Newegg Gamer Zone on July 17.

news.title_en: US probes Samsung for alleged infringement of Netlist's memory-chip patents
news.title_zh: US probes Samsung for alleged infringement of Netlist's memory-chip patents
news.summary_en: U.S. trade regulators have launched a probe into Samsung Electronics' memory chips and products sold by Google, ‌Nvidia, Broadcom and Super Micro Computer that use them following ‌a complaint by Netlist alleging infringement of its patents.  California-based Netlist has accused Samsung and ​its U.S. units of infringing its patents on dynamic random access memory, a type of chip that temporarily stores data for processors and is a critical component in the servers powering the AI boom, the U.S. ‌International Trade Commission said ⁠on Wednesday.  Netlist has asked the USITC to block imports of the disputed chips and products and order the ⁠companies to stop selling them in the U.S. An ITC judge will hold an evidentiary hearing and issue an initial ruling, subject to review ​by the ​commission.
news.summary_zh: U.S. trade regulators have launched a probe into Samsung Electronics' memory chips and products sold by Google, ‌Nvidia, Broadcom and Super Micro Computer that use them following ‌a complaint by Netlist alleging infringement of its patents.  California-based Netlist has accused Samsung and ​its U.S. units of infringing its patents on dynamic random access memory, a type of chip that temporarily stores data for processors and is a critical component in the servers powering the AI boom, the U.S. ‌International Trade Commission said ⁠on Wednesday.  Netlist has asked the USITC to block imports of the disputed chips and products and order the ⁠companies to stop selling them in the U.S. An ITC judge will hold an evidentiary hearing and issue an initial ruling, subject to review ​by the ​commission.
news.context_zh: 📰 来源: AVGO · Yahoo · 英文原文(未翻译)
